coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots tailoring the interfacial proton and charge transfer of iridium nanowires with stress strain for boosting bifunctional hydrogen catalysis
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Qin, Keyang
Zhu, Wenxiang
Wang, Meng
Wu, Jie
Ma, Mengjie
Chen, Jinxin
Liao, Fan
Kang, Zhenhui
Shao, Mingwang
coredata.dc:description: The effective harnessing of hydrogen energy relies on the development of bifunctional electrocatalysts that facilitate hydrogen evolution/oxidation reactions (HER/HOR) with high catalytic activity. The design of such electrocatalysts requires the consideration of not only the volcano relationship with hydrogen binding energy (HBE) or hydrogen adsorption Gibbs free energy (ΔGH) but also the regulation of catalytic kinetics such as interfacial proton/electron transfer. In this work, unique one-dimensional iridium nanowires with compressive stress are successfully prepared and combined with carbon dots (Ir NWs/CDs). Acting as an electrocatalyst for HER in 0.5 M H2SO4, the optimal Ir NWs/CDs only requires an 18 mV overpotential to achieve a current density of −10 mA cm−2. Furthermore, the optimal Ir NWs/CDs shows high HOR performance with a mass activity (@ 50 mV versus RHE) 1.5 times that of 20% Pt/C and excellent anti-CO toxicity ability which is twice the level of the PtRu/C catalyst. Ir NWs/CDs exhibit enhanced HER/HOR activity due to (1) the appropriate modulation of the binding energy to hydrogen intermediate facilitated by the compressive stress applied to the Ir structure and (2) the improved proton/electron transfer kinetics by optimizing the electronic properties and surface structures through tailored CDs. This study delivers a new strategy for designing and synthesizing efficient acidic HER/HOR bifunctional catalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lattice strain
Carbon dots
Hydrogen evolution reaction
Bifunctional catalyst
Hydrogen oxidation reaction

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Ir NWs and IrNWs/CDs
Preparation and structure analysis of Ir NWs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The Characterization of Ir NWs. (a) XRD pattern of Ir NWs-300. (b) SEM image of Ir NWs-300. (c) TEM image of Ir NWs-300. (d) Ir elemental line analysis of Ir NWs-300, insert is the line analysis position. (e) XPS spectrum of Ir 4f peaks for Ir NWs-300. (f) The values of lattice strain with different annealing temperatures.

The Characterization of Ir NWs/CDs-15. (a) XRD patterns of Ir NWs and Ir NWs/CDs-15. (b) SEM image of Ir NWs/CDs-15. (c) TEM image of Ir NWs/CDs-15. (d) The HADDF-STEM image and corresponding element mapping of Ir NWs/CDs-15. (e) HRTEM image of Ir NWs/CDs-15. (f) The corresponding FFT of Ir NWs/CDs-15.

The Characterization of Ir NWs/CDs-15. (a) FT-IR (upper part) and Raman (bottom part) spectra of Ir NWs/CDs-15. (b) XPS spectrum of the C 1s for Ir NWs/CDs-15. (c) XPS spectrum of the O 1s for Ir NWs/CDs-15. (d) XPS spectrum of the Ir 4f for Ir NWs/CDs-15.

The performance of HER. (a) The LSV of Ir NWs/CDs-15, Ir NWs-300, 20% Pt/C, and Ir/C with iR-correction. (b) Tafel plots were calculated from the LSV of Ir NWs/CDs-15, Ir NWs-300, 20% Pt/C, and Ir/C in Fig. 4a. (c) The comparison of Ir NWs/CDs-15 with other recently reported precious metal-based catalysts. (d) Comparison of MA for Ir NWs/CDs-15, Ir NWs, 20% Pt/C, and Ir/C at different potentials against the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE). (e) LSV curves of Ir NWs/CDs-15 pre and post 2000 cycles CV test, scan rate: 5 mV s−1. (f) Chronopotentiometry durability of Ir NWs/CDs-15 and 20% Pt/C at −10 mA cm−2.

HOR performance. (a) The LSV of HOR curves obtained on Ir NWs/CDs-15, 20% Pt/C, and Ir/C. (b) The LSV of HOR curves of Ir NWs/CDs-15 with various rotational speeds. (c) Koutecky-Levich plot of Ir NWs/CDs-15. (d) Linear fitting curves in the micro-polarization regions for both HOR&HER. (The fitting range is the data area contained within the box in Fig. 5d). (e) The HOR&HER Tafel plots of kinetic current density (jk). (f) j0 and MA normalized to metal loading of Ir NWs/CDs-15, 20% Pt/C, and Ir/C. Anti-CO poisoning performance of (g) Ir NWs/CDs-15; (h) 20% Pt/C; and (i) Ir/C. Error bars are means ± SD (n = 3 replicates).

head.ce:title: Carbon dots tailoring the interfacial proton and charge transfer of iridium nanowires with stress strain for boosting bifunctional hydrogen catalysis

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University, 199 Ren'ai Road, Suzhou 215123, Jiangsu, China (Z. Kang).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM)
Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots modified iridium nanowires (Ir NWs/CDs) show high catalytic activity and stability for acidic hydrogen evolution/oxidation reactions, which are attributed to the large compression stress generated in Ir NWs and the fast proton/electron transfer ability and good conductivity of CDs.

The effective harnessing of hydrogen energy relies on the development of bifunctional electrocatalysts that facilitate hydrogen evolution/oxidation reactions (HER/HOR) with high catalytic activity. The design of such electrocatalysts requires the consideration of not only the volcano relationship with hydrogen binding energy (HBE) or hydrogen adsorption Gibbs free energy (ΔGH) but also the regulation of catalytic kinetics such as interfacial proton/electron transfer. In this work, unique one-dimensional iridium nanowires with compressive stress are successfully prepared and combined with carbon dots (Ir NWs/CDs). Acting as an electrocatalyst for HER in 0.5 M H2SO4, the optimal Ir NWs/CDs only requires an 18 mV overpotential to achieve a current density of −10 mA cm−2. Furthermore, the optimal Ir NWs/CDs shows high HOR performance with a mass activity (@ 50 mV versus RHE) 1.5 times that of 20% Pt/C and excellent anti-CO toxicity ability which is twice the level of the PtRu/C catalyst. Ir NWs/CDs exhibit enhanced HER/HOR activity due to (1) the appropriate modulation of the binding energy to hydrogen intermediate facilitated by the compressive stress applied to the Ir structure and (2) the improved proton/electron transfer kinetics by optimizing the electronic properties and surface structures through tailored CDs. This study delivers a new strategy for designing and synthesizing efficient acidic HER/HOR bifunctional catalysts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The escalating energy crisis and pollution issues have disrupted the carbon equilibrium on the earth [1]. Replacing traditional fossil fuels with renewable energy sources is of paramount importance in addressing these issues [2-4]. Hydrogen energy is of great significance for achieving carbon neutrality and has received widespread attention as a renewable energy technology [5]. To efficiently employ hydrogen, hydrogen catalytic reactions have been profoundly studied, including hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) and hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). In acid water splitting reaction, HER has high efficiency; while in fuel cells, HOR in proton-exchange membrane fuel cells demonstrates two orders of magnitude higher activity on Pd, Ir, and Pt catalysts compared to that exchange membrane fuel cells in alkaline. Pt metal is considered as an advanced electrocatalyst for HOR/HER. However, the poor stability in HER and the lack of CO anti-poisoning performance in HOR of Pt are still the main obstacles to performance improvement. Hence, it is imperative to design bifunctional electrocatalysts that maintain stable high catalytic activity for both the HER and HOR under acidic conditions, to facilitate the efficient utilization of hydrogen energy.

The catalytic process adheres to the Sabatier principle, which exhibits a volcano relationship. For HER/HOR, this principle emphasizes the significance of metal catalysts positioned close to the apex of the volcanic curve, possessing suitable hydrogen binding energy (HBE) or hydrogen adsorption Gibbs free energy (ΔGH), as they exhibit good HER/HOR activity. From the volcano graph of computed hydrogen adsorption energies, iridium (Ir) has been considered one of the most favorable metals in the HOR/HER reaction [6]. Nonetheless, due to the limited adsorption capacity of Ir for hydrogen (Had) in acidic media, its HER/HOR performance is not satisfactory. It is an effective approach to optimize the binding energy between reaction intermediates and the surface of the catalyst to promote H-related catalysis [7]. In the pursuit of electrocatalysts toward promoting the HER and HOR of Ir-based catalysts, considerable endeavors have been dedicated to elevating the catalytic activity while simultaneously reducing costs. For example, changing the electronic structure of the material to achieve equilibrium adsorption of HER/HOR intermediaries; designing catalysts with dispersed active sites to adjust their adsorption capacity for intermediates to improve catalytic activity, and adjusting the magnitude of lattice strain can be employed to modify the surface electronic structure [8-11].

In addition to the Sabatier principle, the role of interfacial conditions between catalysts and electrocatalytic media has been gradually recognized recently, which also regulates the catalytic activity. Surface modification of electrocatalysts enhances the interaction of electrons between different substances, promoting electron transfer, and additionally enhancing the catalytic performance of composites. The selection of co-catalyst is of great importance, as it impacts not only the structure of the active sites but also thecorresponding electrocatalytic performance of the catalysts. Carbon dots (CDs), with particle size smaller than 10 nm and abundant oxygen-containing functional groups, are frequently employed as co-catalysts. Furthermore, CDs can both enrich H+ and function as electron acceptors and donors, a critical factor in enhancing hydrogen catalytic efficiency [12-16]. Such critical behaviors endow CDs ability in enhancing hydrogen catalytic efficiency, optimizing the adsorption of the reaction intermediates, and finally promoting the hydrogen catalytic processes [17-21].

In this work, CDs modified Ir nanowires with compressive strain (Ir NWs/CDs-x) were synthesized. Ir NWs/CDs-15 is used as an effective catalyst for acidic HER and HOR due to the compression stress of Ir NWs generated during the preparation process and the unique interfacial properties of CDs including fast proton/electron transfer and good conductivity. Ir NWs/CDs-15 displays outstanding acidic HER activity with an extraordinarily low overpotential of 18 mV at −10 mA cm−2, which is much lower than that of the commercial Ir/C, and 20% Pt/C catalysts. And the Tafel slope of Ir NWs/CDs-15 is 20 mV dec−1, surpassing that of the references’ catalysts. In HOR, Ir NWs/CDs-15 also displays excellent CO tolerance in acidic HOR. After 1800 s of anti-poisoning testing, its HOR performance only decreased by 11.8%, which is superior to the PtRu/C catalyst.

Iridium trichloride (IrCl3, 99.9%), potassium hydroxide (KOH, 99%), commercial iridium oxide (C-IrO2, 99%), Ir/C (Ir of 20 wt%), PtRu/C (PtRu/C with Pt of 20 wt%, Ru of 10 wt%), graphite rods (99.99%), toray carbon paper (TCP-H-60) were used in the experiment. The water used in this work was ultrapure water (The resistance is approximately equal to 18.2 MΩ cm−1). The reagents used were analytical-grade reagents.

The CDs were obtained by the electrolysis method, where graphite rods were used as carbon source. During electrolysis, a direct current regulated power supply is used as energy source, the voltage is fixed at 30 V and the electrolyte is pure water. After the electrolysis process lasted for 20 days, the black solution was collected and centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 15 min to get rid of the large carbon particles. Lastly, the black solution was freeze-drying to obtain CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Ir NWs and IrNWs/CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The Ir NWs-300 was obtained by directly annealing IrO2 nanowires (IrO2 NWs) at 300 °C in Ar/H2 (95 wt% Ar, 5 wt%H2) atmosphere for 2 h. IrO2 NWs were synthesized according to the literature [22]. Different amounts of CDs were added to Ir NWs-300. The CDs were obtained by the electrolysis method [23-25]. Ir NWs-300 and CDs powder were mixed evenly, frozen dry, and then calcined at 200 °C for 2 h under an Ar atmosphere. Catalysts with different composition contents were obtained by adding mass fractions of 5, 10, 15, and 20% of CDs to Ir NWs-300 and named as IrNWs/CDs-x, where × means the mass fractions of CDs.

The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) was used to reveal the phase and crystallography of materials. The sample morphologies and structures were critically characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, with an instrument model of Zeiss G500), transmission electron microscope (TEM, with an instrument model of FEI Talos F200X). The Raman spectra were recorded by a Raman spectrometer with 633 nm laser wavelength. The valence states of elements were determined through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS).

Rotating ring-disk electrodes (RRDE) loaded with catalysts are employed as working electrodes. The electrochemical test was performed with the CHI660 electrochemical workstation. The saturated calomel electrode (SCE) electrodes employed in the experiment were calibrated in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and structure analysis of Ir NWs
ce:section.ce:para: The Ir NWs were obtained by directly annealing the monoclinic phase IrO2 in an Ar/H2 atmosphere and the corresponding synthetic schematic is displayed in Fig. S1. The monoclinic phase IrO2 precursor was synthesized following the procedure documented in the literature [22]. The corresponding XRD pattern is shown in Fig. S2a, the same as the literature reports. SEM and TEM images of monoclinic phase IrO2 clearly show its nanowire morphology (Fig. S2b and S2c). The energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) mapping images illustrate that the elemental Ir and O are evenly distributed within the monoclinic phase IrO2, as depicted in Fig. S2d. The XPS measurement results of IrO2 NWs are illustrated in Fig. S3. The full XPS spectrum (Fig. S3a) indicates that IrO2 NWs contain only Ir and O elements, and the Ir core spectrum shows that the valence state of Ir species was +4 (Fig. S3b).

After calcining at 300 °C in an Ar/H2 atmosphere for 2 h, Ir NWs-300 was obtained. The XRD pattern (Fig. 1a) indicates that Ir NWs-300 is composed of high-purity of Ir (JCPDS No. 46-1044). SEM and TEM images of Ir NWs-300 display its nanowires morphology (Fig. 1 b and 1c), which originates from the morphology of the monoclinic phase IrO2 precursor. The TEM image is shown in Fig. S4a, and the corresponding EDX mapping image show that Ir is uniformly distributed in Ir NWs-300 (Fig. S4b). The EDX line analyses of Ir NWs-300 (Fig. 1d) indicate that the oxygen element has been almost removed. Fig. 1e shows the Ir 4f XPS spectrum of Ir NWs-300, and the Ir 4f XPS spectrum with peak positions at 60.8 eV and 63.8 eV are assigned to elemental Ir for the 4f7/2 and 4f5/2, respectively [26].

As demonstrated in Fig. S5b (a partially enlarged view of Fig. S5a), the XRD characterization of Ir NWs-300 reveals a noticeable rightward shift when compared to the Ir standard card (JCDPS No. 46-1044). The rightward shift of the peak indicates the presence of a compressive strain. To assess the impact of heat treatment temperatures on compressive strain strength, monoclinic phase IrO2 was subjected to heat treatment at temperatures of 200, 300, 400, and 500 °C, resulting in the formation of Ir NWs-200, Ir NWs-300, Ir NWs-400, and Ir NWs-500, respectively. Additionally, the compressive strain strength of Ir NWs with various heat treatment temperatures was determined using the Williamson-Hall equation (Fig. S6) [27-30]. The values are presented in Fig. 1f, revealing that Ir NWs-300 exhibits the highest compressive strain strength at −0.563%, surpassing the other samples. To provide further evidence of the presence of stress and to precisely determine the local compressive strain strength, the atomic arrangement of Ir NWs obtained by reduction at different temperatures was revealed through high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) images (Fig. S7a-d). Due to the presence of compressive strain, the lattice spacing of the sample is different (Fig. S7e-h). The d-average spacing of the (111) crystal planes in the compression zone of Ir NWs-300 is 2.06 Å, which is smaller than Ir NWs-200 (2.14 Å), Ir NWs-400 (2.12 Å), and Ir NWs-500 (2.17 Å), indicating that Ir NWs-300 has the maximum compressive strain. It's noteworthy that the d-spacing of the uncompressed Ir (111) crystal plane is 2.21 Å. Therefore, the local maximum compressive strain strength of Ir NWs-300 could potentially reach up to −6.8% ((2.06–2.21) × 100%/2.21 = -6.8%). To verify that compressive stress is generated during the synthesis process, we also characterized commercial Ir powder for comparison. Fig. S8a shows the XRD spectrum of commercial Ir powder. After further calculation of its stress intensity, it was found that the compressive strain strength of commercial Ir powder was only −0.082% (Fig. S8b), which is much lower than the samples obtained at Ir NWs-300 (-0.563%) and other temperatures.

In Fig. S9a, the XRD pattern of CDs displays a broad peak at approximately 22.6°. CDs are observed to exist in an almost monodispersed state, with a mean particle size of approximately 2.4 nm, as clearly depicted in Fig. S9d. In the HRTEM (Fig. S9c), a lattice spacing of approximately 0.210 nm can be observed, and this crystal plane is corresponded to the (100) plane of graphite. The particle size distribution of the synthesized CDs, as shown in Fig. S9d, exhibits a high degree of uniformity. The FT-IR and Raman spectra of CDs (Fig. S10) are in accordance with those reported in literature, confirming the successful synthesis of CDs [15]. According to the added amount of CDs in Ir NWs-300, the catalysts were named as Ir NWs/CDs-x (x is the mass fraction of CDs in the catalyst). The XRD pattern of Ir NWs/CDs-15 is shown in Fig. 2 a. The peaks located at 40.8°, 47.2°, 69.2°, 83.5°, and 88.2° match well with the standard card of Ir (JCDPS No. 46-1044). A wide peak appeared at 22.3° corresponds to the XRD peak of CDs, which demonstrates the combination of CDs with Ir NWs in Ir NWs/CDs-15. As shown in Fig. 2b, the SEM image reveals that Ir NWs/CDs-15 remains a nanowire morphology. Similarly, the SEM image of Ir NWs/CDs-5, 10, and 20 (Fig. S11) display similar nanowire morphology with Ir NWs/CDs-15. TEM (Fig. 2c) image was employed to reveal the morphology of Ir NWs/CDs-15. The HADDF-STEM image and corresponding element mapping of Ir NWs/CDs-15 are shown in Fig. 2d, where Ir, C, and O are evenly distributed on the nanowires. The HRTEM image of Ir NWs/CDs-15 is presented in Fig. 2e. The lattice spacing of 0.220 nm and 0.192 nm corresponds to the (111) and (200) planes of Ir, respectively. The lattice strain can also be observed in a high-resolution image in Fig. 2e, and the corresponding lattice fringes undergo significant bending. In addition, CDs were also observed near Ir NWs, corresponding to a lattice fringe spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) plane of CDs, demonstrating the successful binding of CDs and Ir-NWs. The related fast-Fourier transform (FFT) pattern shows the nanoscale crystalline structure of the Ir NWs/CDs-15 (Fig. 2f).

The Raman and FT-IR spectra of Ir NWs/CDs-15 are shown in Fig. 3 a (upper part). The absorption peaks at 1210, 1400, 1600, 3130, and 3440 cm−1 correspond to COC, CC, CO, CH, and OH vibrations, respectively [31]. The abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CDs can enhance the mass transfer capability of the catalyst, thereby improving its catalytic performance. In the Raman spectrum of Ir NWs/CDs-15 (Fig. 3a bottom part), two vibration absorption peaks at 1400 and 1650 cm−1 can be observed, and these are attributed to the D and G bands [23,32]. The XPS of Ir NWs/CDs-15 shows the C 1s spectrum (Fig. 3b) with distinct peaks at 288.9, 286.7, and 284.8 eV, corresponding to CO, CO, and CC, respectively, in agreement with the FT-IR results [18]. The presence of CO and CO peaks, observed at 532.1 and 533.4 eV, respectively, is shown in the O 1s spectrum of Ir NWs/CDs-15 in Fig. 3c [18]. Fig. 3d displays the Ir 4f spectrum of Ir NWs/CDs-15, with peaks at 60.8 eV and 63.8 eV attributed to Ir 4f7/2 and 4f5/2 respectively [33]. The negative migration of 0.1 eV may be due to electron transfer from CDs to Ir in Ir NWs, indicating that CDs have successfully recombined with Ir NWs.

The HER and HOR electrochemical performance of Ir-NWs/CDs was measured in a three-electrode system using 0.5 M H2SO4 saturated with H2 as the electrolyte. Prior to the electrochemical test, calibration of the SCE was performed (Fig. S12). To facilitate comparison, we evaluated the HER performance of Ir NWs obtained at various temperatures in a 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. Fig. S13 illustrates the HER polarization curves of these catalysts, namely Ir NWs-200, Ir NWs-300, Ir NWs-400, and Ir NWs-500. Among the four catalysts, Ir NWs-300 exhibits the most favorable HER activity, suggesting that electrocatalytic performance can be progressively enhanced with increased compressive strain.

After adding CDs on this basis, the HER performance of Ir NWs/CDs-15 has been further improved. When compared to commercial Ir NWs-300, commercial Ir/C, and 20% Pt/C catalysts, Ir NWs/CDs-15 exhibited the highest activity, as illustrated in Fig. 4 a. Specifically, Ir NWs/CDs-15 recorded an overpotential of 18 mV @ −10 mA cm−2, which is significantly better than that of Ir/C (39 mV), 20% Pt/C (30 mV), and Ir NWs-300 (29 mV). Fig. 4b shows the Tafel slope for Ir NWs/CDs-15 was determined to be 20 mV dec−1, which is notably lower than that of 20% Pt/C (29 mV dec−1), Ir/C (40 mV dec−1), and Ir NWs-300 (26 mV dec−1). The low Tafel slope of Ir NWs/CDs-15 leads to a rapid increase with rising current density. This characteristic is highly advantageous for practical applications. In addition, the overpotential (@ −10 mA cm−2) and the Tafel slope of Ir-NWs/CDs-15 were compared with other highly active precious metal-based catalysts for HER (Fig. 4c and Table S1), indicating that Ir-NWs/CDs-15 is much better than that of the most reported advanced Ir based electrocatalysts. The mass activity (MA) of various electrocatalysts is shown in Fig. 4d. Specifically, the MA of Ir NWs/CDs-15 may reach 1271 A gIr −1 (@ 0.1 V versus RHE). This value represents an enhancement of 1.2, 1.5, and 1.8 times in comparison to the corresponding values for 20% Pt/C (1075 A gPt −1), Ir/C (877 A gIr −1), and Ir NWs-300 (705 A gIr −1), respectively. The electrochemically active surface areas (ECSA) of various catalysts were decided by integrating the hydrogen adsorption charge obtained from the cyclic voltammogram (CV) curves, as illustrated in Fig. S14. The ECSAs of Ir NWs, Ir NWs/CDs-15, and 20% Pt/C were calculated as 23.1, 35.5, and 59.5 m2 g−1, respectively.

The HER activity of four Ir NWs/CDs catalysts with different amounts of CDs was tested with the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves (Fig. S15a). Ir NWs/CDs-15 presents the most outstanding catalytic activity out of the four samples tested. More importantly, as the CDs content in the catalyst increases, the Tafel slope (Fig. S15b) gradually decreases, which means that CDs can accelerate the kinetic process of HER reaction. However, with the increase of CDs contents, the overpotential of the composite first increases and then decreases, which indicates that only adding an appropriate amount of CDs can promote the occurrence of catalytic reaction.

The stability of Ir NWs/CDs-15 for HER was assessed through 2000 cycles of CV tests, as depicted in Fig. S16. The LSV curves were compared before and after the long-term experiment (Fig. 4e). Remarkably, the overpotential of Ir NWs/CDs-15 exhibited a mere 2 mV increase @ −10 mA cm−2 after testing (Fig. S17). This finding underscores the robust stability of Ir NWs/CDs-15 in the HER. Furthermore, the chronopotentiometry test was performed to evaluate the sustained performance of Ir NWs/CDs-15 catalysts under a fixed current density and to make comparisons with commercial catalysts. The catalytic activity of 20% Pt/C decayed slowly with time (Fig. 4f). In sharp contrast, the Ir NWs/CDs-15 exhibited durable stability toward the HER, which maintained nearly constant voltage under −10 mA cm−2 during the 60 h stability test. The characterization of the Ir NWs/CDs-15 after the stability test (Fig. S18) showed that the change in crystallinity of the Ir NWs/CDs-15 before and after the stability test was negligible. The XPS characterization results before and after the stability test (Fig. S18d) showed that after long-term stability testing, the binding energy of the Ir 4f orbitals of Ir NWs/CDs-15 shifted to the right by 0.05 eV. This implies that the valence state of the Ir NWs/CDs-15 catalyst remained largely unchanged pre and post the stability test.

To further assess the electrochemical performance of Ir NWs/CDs-15, we investigated the catalytic properties of the Ir NWs/CDs-15 and other catalysts toward anodic HOR. Figs. S19a and S19b show the CV curves of Ir NWs/CDs-15 and 20% Pt/C in which a large HOR current can be observed. Firstly, Ir NWs samples with compressive stress obtained at different temperatures were assessed by the RRDE method within a three-electrode configuration in H2-saturated 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. (Fig. S20a). The HOR activity of the catalyst exhibits an increase with the growth of compressive strain. The hydrogen oxidation ability of four Ir NWs/CDs catalysts with different amounts was tested with the polarization curves as depicted in Fig. S20b. Ir NWs/CDs-15 exhibits the best HOR performance. The LSV curves of Ir/C, 20% Pt/C and Ir NWs/CDs-15 are acquired under 1600 rpm on an RRDE (Fig. 5a). The activity of HOR for Ir NWs/CDs-15 was remarkably higher than those of Ir/C, and 20% Pt/C. It is worth mentioning that the j at η = 25 mV of Ir NWs/CDs-15 is in the top level of HOR catalysts (Table S2). Among all these samples, Ir NWs/CDs-15 has the highest anode current density. We also conducted the HOR polarization curve tests on Ir NWs/CDs-15, 20% Pt/C and Ir/C at various rotating speeds (Fig. 5 b, S21a and S21c). As illustrated in Fig. 5b, the limit current density rises with the augmentation of rotational speed. This enhancement in current density is attributed to the improved surface quality transfer. Diffusion limits the ultimate current density at a positive potential. Fig. 5c demonstrates the Koutecky–Levich plots, illustrating a linear correlation between the reciprocal of the current density and the reciprocal of the square root of the rotational rate. The slope of the Koutecky-Levich plot for Ir NWs/CDs-15 was determined to be 12.07 cm2 mA−1 rpm1/2 at 50 mV, a value close to the theoretical value (14.80 cm2 mA−1 rpm1/2) [34]. Furthermore, the HOR polarization curves at various rotating speeds for 20% Pt/C and Ir/C, along with their corresponding Koutecky–Levich plots, are presented in Fig. S21b and S21d, in which the slopes for both 20% Pt/C and Ir/C match closely with the theoretical standard. The kinetic current density (jk) of the Ir NWs/CDs-15 catalyst was subsequently calculated using the Koutecky–Levich equation. The Ir NWs/CDs-15 catalyst attained a jk value of 22.4 mA cm−2 for the HOR (@ 50 mV versus RHE). This value is approximately 1.5 times greater than that of 20% Pt/C (14.8 mA cm−2), and 1.8 times greater than that of Ir/C (12.2 mA cm−2). Subsequently, we determined the exchange current density (j0) for the investigated catalysts by conducting linear fitting within the micro-polarization regions (-1 to 1 mV), as depicted in Fig. 5d. The Ir NWs/CDs-15 catalyst shows a j0 of 3.40 mA cm−2, surpassing that of 20% Pt/C (2.5 mA cm−2), and Ir/C (1.8 mA cm−2), as depicted in Fig. 5f (left). Following that, we calculated the relationship between the jk of Ir NWs/CDs-15, Ir/C, and 20% Pt/C catalysts as a logarithmic function and the potential within the Tafel region, as illustrated in Fig. 5e. For precise comparisons, the MA at a potential of 50 mV is normalized to the noble metal loading. As shown in Fig. 5f (right), the Ir NWs/CDs-15 has the highest MA of 0.7 A mgmetal −2, which is 1.5 and 1.8 folds than 20% Pt/C and Ir/C.

In fuel cells, catalysts made of Pt group metals at the anode are susceptible to poisoning by impurity gases, primarily CO, presented in the hydrogen fuel. This poisoning is due to the preferential combination of CO with the active site exposed by the metal, thus blocking the sites of hydrogen adsorption and dissociation [35-37]. Therefore, it is imperative to assess the anti-CO toxicity ability of catalysts during HOR. Firstly, the LSV curves of Ir NWs/CDs-15 were compared under H2 and H2/1% CO atmosphere, and its HOR performance decreased by 5.9% in the presence of CO (Fig. 5g). Contrastingly, when subjected to the same condition, the performance of Ir/C and 20% Pt/C decreased by 17.2% and 11.0%, respectively, as shown in Fig. 5i and 5 h. Furthermore, the durability of catalysts was assessed through chronoamperometry at 0.10 V versus RHE in H2-saturated 0.5 M H2SO4 solution containing 1% CO. As depicted in Fig. S22, the relative current density for HOR on Ir NWs/CDs-15 exhibited a decrease of 11.8% after 1800 s of operation, which is significantly lower than that of the reference PtRu/C electrocatalyst, which decreased by 23.4%, indicating that it is a high-efficiency antipoisoning catalyst [38]. In addition, we also tested the HOR polarization curve of PtRu/C (Fig. S23). Under long-term chronopotentiometry testing, the 20% Pt/C catalyst lost its HOR catalytic activity in a very short period. These test results reveal that Ir NWs/CDs-15 possess excellent resistance to CO poisoning, potentially attributable to the adsorption of CO molecules by CDs, which subsequently safeguard the active sites of the catalyst surface.

The improvement effect of stress on performance is prominent. The compressive strain intensity of Ir NWs-300 is up to −0.563%. Previous reports have shown that compressive strain can adjust the hydrogen absorption energy of Ir to the top of the volcanic map, thereby improving the performance of HER and HOR simultaneously.

In addition, the introduction of CDs greatly improves catalytic activity. CDs have the functions of electron donors and acceptors, which can quickly store and release electrons. At the same time, CDs have good adsorption effects on H+ and water. Measurement of the H+ capture ability of CDs (Fig. S24) revealed that they captured 73.4 mmol g−1H+ within 60 min. More importantly, within the first 10 min, the H+ capture capacity of CDs reached 42.5 mmol g−1, demonstrating their excellent ability to adsorb H+ quickly. In electrocatalysis, mass transfer transpires at the three-phase interface involving solid, liquid, and gas on the catalyst surface. Consequently, the wettability of catalysts is a crucial parameter for assessing their capacity for mass transfer [39]. CDs exhibit notable hydrophilicity due to its abundant oxygen-containing functional groups. Press Ir NWs/CDs-15 and Ir NWs into tablets, and subsequently, the contact angle of each tablet was measured, as shown in Fig. S25. Research on the static contact angle of water droplets revealed that the contact angle of Ir NWs/CDs-15 (21°) is smaller than that of Ir NWs (30°), suggesting that Ir NWs/CDs-15 has better hydrophilicity than Ir NWs. The improved wettability of the electrode surface can promote the rapid penetration of electrolytes and accelerate the release of products, thereby enhancing reaction kinetics [40]. Liquid droplets can infiltrate Ir NWs/CDs-15 catalysts containing CDs more rapidly. This phenomenon suggests that the oxygen-containing functional groups on CDs surface facilitate electrical contact between the conductive substrate and the electrolyte. Enhanced hydrophilicity corresponds to an accelerated mass transfer rate between the catalyst surface and electrolyte, which is pivotal for achieving superior catalytic performance. Therefore, throughout HER/HOR processes, CDs act as a bridge connecting hydrogen proton and electron with Ir active sites. During hydrogen evolution, the electrolyte can quickly contact the Ir active site through the CDs, and the electrons can also be quickly transferred to the Ir site through the CDs, improving the kinetic process of the reaction. In the HOR process, a large amount of H+ is generated at the Ir active sites which will be captured by CDs, thus enhancing the diffusion ability of H+ on the metal surface and consequently promoting the HOR performance.

In summary, we synthesized iridium nanowires/carbon dots (Ir NWs/CDs) as highly active and durable bifunctional hydrogen evolution/oxidation reaction (HER/HOR) catalysts. The synergistic combination of compressively strained Ir NWs and CDs promotes the catalytic process in the two reactions. Ir-NWs/CDs-15 exhibits excellent HER performance in a 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolyte, with a mere overpotential of 18 mV to achieve −10 mA cm−2. In addition, the mass activity (@ 50 mV versus RHE) of Ir-NWs/CDs-15 in acidic electrolyte is 1.5 times to that of 20% Pt/C for HOR. The Ir-NWs/CDs-15 catalyst also exhibits excellent anti-CO poisoning ability for HOR. The outstanding catalytic activity for hydrogen reactions comes from the dual contributions of compressive strain applied to the Ir NWs and the H+ concentration enriching ability of the CDs. We believe that the integrated effects of compressive stress and CDs within a single catalyst hold great promise for advancing hydrogen energy applications.

Keyang Qin: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Wenxiang Zhu: Investigation. Meng Wang: Investigation. Jie Wu: Investigation. Mengjie Ma: Investigation. Jinxin Chen: Investigation. Fan Liao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Zhenhui Kang: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Mingwang Shao: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.
